title: 2025 07 16 RR
displayTitle: 2025-07-16 - Regulations Review Committee - Briefing on regulatory reform
description: Wednesday 16 July 2025 Regulations Review Committee Briefing on regulatory reform Hearing of Evidence Sir Geoffrey Palmer
descriptionSummary: Briefing on regulatory reform. Sir Geoffrey Palmer

chunks.text: You agree? If she wishes, just take away question from us. Yeah, fair enough. Very good. Okay, I'll, um, I'll take questions away from Lauren. Well, they, they're on the same, Same footing. So Jeffrey, welcome you. Thank you very much for coming and, um, helping us to piece through, um, our own thinking as a committee on this.

Uh, I'll hand over to you, but as you can see, um, a number of committee members have, uh, questions for you. So you ask time for that. Well, um, I, I, you were all young long ago when I was in this parliament, these questions of regulation were begg in those days.

We had the economic stabilization Act of 1948 that allowed everything that moved to be regulated, uh, without resort to parliament at all. Uh, and that was done as, as Sir Robert Muldoon once famously told the times of London, if you hang your hat on economic stabilization, you can do anything.

And he did. We had all wages regulated at one stage, all interest rates and even carless days. Now, you are all too young to remember that, but I can tell you that is executive government, the like, of which we've never seen before. That led me in the second edition of Unbridled Power that was published in 19 and 97 to cause those laws that we had.

We, we had made lot of use of delegated legislation for many years. The distinction that was made between before primary legislation and delegated legislation was secondary legislation was vital to this whole discussion. And I was very pleased to hear the legislation design committee.

I, I chaired the pre it's pre desist of that committee. Um, and, um, when I was at the Law Commission, now, let me say that the Economic Stabilization Act, the Commerce Amendment Act of 1979 and various other, the National Development Act that allowed you to develop New Zealand by order and council and not by parliament.

These were very grave exercises of executive power. And that led to the repeal of all those statutes. And it also led to the setting up on this committee, which was part of the Labor Government's policy for the 1984 election. And the committee got to work and was pretty active for a long time.

And it had bipartisan support. And when this, I won't characterize it in unfair terms, but with the regulatory standards bill appeared in this parliament, it was exceedingly difficult to understand what it was going to try and achieve because it seems, uh, if you look at the 75 page regulatory impact statement, you find even that the officials of the minister's own department didn't agree with the bill.

And indeed, one of the reasons why they might not have, there was nothing said about the Regulations Review committee in, in the legislation, or indeed, as far as I can see in any of the consideration that led to the drafting of this Ill considered Bill. Now, the, the truth of the matter is that the disallowance of regulations, which is what the aim of this legislation, the regulatory standards bill purpose, is to reduce the amount of legislation, not directly, but indirectly, by producing a lot of reports

that don't have legal effect, but which are given to people who do have decision making capacity. Uh, and, and of course that is, uh, a bit like the Chinese water torture. You will have a lot of reports coming and you'll get nervous about it if you're a minister. And indeed, it, the, the, the effect of the, of the bill, uh, is to, it's, it's actually designed to split ministerial responsibility.

Ministers are responsible for the secondary legislation that they administer from the primary act. And I think the suggestion that the, this leg, that the regulatory standards will be restricted to primary legislation's, entirely sound, otherwise, you're gonna have a confused miss.

The, the conduct of this parliament already pretty unsatisfactory in many points of view, uh, is going to get a whole lot worse when you have this, these confusing areas of responsibility that don't fit. And I thought the previous speaker was extremely tactile the way he put that, but I'm telling you that confusion is the worst thing that you want.

And and I, I have to say that the power to disallow is not exercised very often. And one of the things that worries me about that is it leads to people saying that regulations are too frequent. Because when they are made, they're not disallowed as the Parliament has the power to do and has had for many years.

Uh, that was another part of the Labor Party policy in my day to set up the Regulations Disallowance Act, and it's not used much. And why isn't it? I'll tell you the reason. There's a lot of jurisprudence with the regulation, with the Regulations Review Committee. It's even published at the university.

It's been over a period of years, developed quite well. The bad habits of New Zealand legislation have been somewhat restricted, uh, by the activities of this committee, but not enough. Now, the difficulty with the, with the new bill is that it promises far too much and it cannot deliver it because it's a vast amount of, let me just go through the amount of law that we now have on the books.

It's astonishing. We have, um, uh, amount of law, which, uh, which the Parliamentary Council has, uh, has said is it's 2 5 4 2 5 4 5 8 2 3 4 pages of primary legislation. That's millions. It's 25 million that comes from Parliamentary Council now,

and most of it is, uh, public act. Although there's a whole lot of provincial legisl, uh, provincial council stuffs still on the books. If you're gonna get rid of legislation, you have to have a policy of doing it. And it's very time consuming, and that's why it's not done enough.

And to, to remove unnecessary statutes from the book, you need to give a job to the Law commission and get them to research whether they're still needed, what the legal effects would be if they were not, and get rid of them. But that has never been done because New Zealand, which loves passing law, even when it's not necessary, uh, wants to pass more law all the time, but never get rid of the law that it has.

And that leads in an old democracy like this to a considerable set of problems that you have to manage and they are unmanageable. Mm-hmm. And, and if this, if this legislation of from the regulatory standards bill goes, what I don't know how the legislation advisory committee is, is, um, I, I don't know how the regulatory standards bill is ever going to coordinate with you.
It's impossible. Mm-hmm. Now, that leads me to this.

If the aim is to curb primary legislation, that is one thing. If the aim is to curb secondary legislation, that is another thing. And I think the secondary legislation is the one that will create the most confusion because of its new, the number of regulations that are made it, I haven't given, given the figures for that.

They're enormous. You cannot run a country on the basis of primary legislation alone. It is not possible. And the ministers have to be able to have the ability to have administrative arrangements that are within the competence of the enabling provisions in the primary act that allows detail to be dealt with, because you can't do everything by primary legislation.

So I just think that the disallowance provisions are highly relevant to the whole enterprise of the regulatory, uh, standards bill, and, and they need to be made part of it. And, and if you, if you are left alone, that would be good. But what you need to do is to be more muscular.

If, if we are going to have economists complaining without any evidence being profit, that there is too much regulation, then you are the people to stop it. The reason it isn't stopped is that ministers don't like that because it means that they're not able to, to, uh, do what they want to do.

And ministers like to be able to do what they want to do because they think that's why they're there. And that's true. So, so you see, I, I just can't see, I can't make any sense of the mess that is now in front of you in this parliament. It is absolutely irresponsible if, if, if, if there is not some policy changes made to try and actually deal with the problem, not the Ima imagined problem.

The Parliament has the power right now in the legislation that is, that used to be the Regulations Disallowance Act, but which is now contained in the legislation Act 2019 part four, to disallow a whole range of statutory regulations. Now, you know better than I, you don't recommend it very often.

I saw you did it the other day for a legal, a legal, um, education partly deserved it, um, about legal education. The more Parliament keeps out of legal educ the better. Uh, but let me say that, uh, I, I would say that there is, there is just a lot of work to revise what is being done here.

It's not to create a great big mess and part of the mess is for you. Yeah. And, and so I, I just, uh, wanted to give you that background because having worked in this area and regarding it regarded as important, uh, important long ago, it somehow hasn't stuck.

Uh, the idea that these these powers have never exercised worries me. Why would Parliament give powers to itself to disallow a whole lot of stuff and never, never do it? And that might make the economist of this world who don't have a good understanding of law better might make them feel better about the Parliament.

There are four questions already for you. Can we move Right where you go First? I'll come to Joseph Mooney. Oh, Thank you, sir Jeffrey, uh, for that, um, uh, submission and, and, uh, for that background as well. Uh, and, uh, I think, uh, you touched on an interesting point that's I've reflect on that we come to this place to be more makers.

Yes. Therefore, there's our metric of success, but not more pruners. Yes, Yes, absolutely. Point, because There's an interesting point there. Um, and you, you, you may or may not be pleased to know that, uh, I did recently file that motion of disallowance, which was debate in the house. It was disallowed. So, so, but they could do that. You could do it all the time.

Well, there's, so don't you. We'll take it on board. Yeah. I Love it. Now, I'm, I'm interested with your, uh, your, your understanding of the, the background of the history of this place, and particularly the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act. Yeah. That you have great familiarity with. And some of the, we've heard from, uh, legislation advisor Design Advising committee.

It's, um, the, the jurisprudence that's developed that was unexpected at the time that the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act was put in place. Now, interesting thing, the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act creates a number of principles or rules that we want society to Yeah. Abide by. Uh, but it also has an Attorney general needs provide a report on Yes.

Back to Parliament. But Parliament can ignore that and it regularly does across governments. Yes. Uh, so with this bill, um, that we now considering this and its intersection with our committee, I'm interested in your thoughts about, uh, how Juris may or may not develop over time, um, given what happened with the New Rising and, and the reporting function that gives to Parliament, but also the ability to parliament to ignore that reporting function.

Yeah. So we're having a, I don't know, what's the 25th anniversary? The Got Rights Act in Canterbury in 1st of October. And it was, they asked me to tell, tell 'em what I thought. I think it should have been entrenched, and I still think that Mm. And, um, if you're going to have human rights, then you should honor them.

And, and the idea that you're going to be able to, uh, somehow slough off it because of the, uh, what the features of the world rights that you've mentioned, I don't agree with that. I think in the small democracy with the executive, as powerful as we have, and that cannot ever be fixed unless the Parliament is bigger.

And that's not a popular thing to do. The theory of Westminster government is that the cabinet is responsible to the House of Representatives, but the House of Representatives is so small that you have ministers chair chairing select committees when they shouldn't.

And we stopped doing that. And, and now it started again because you've got an executive that's got about 23 members in it. Um, and, and, and so it's, it's just, it's just people just want to be ministers. They don't want to be back benches here. They don't want to do the work of, of scrutinizing because it's not sexy politically and you don't have power.

It just happens to be very important. And, and these fundamentals of Westminster government are entirely, um, absent from the New Zealand political psyche. Uh, and, and this is a very small country, and, and it's gonna get itself into big trouble if it goes on the way it is with the way it legislate.

Don, I'll just supplementary. Okay. Just, just on on that though, the, the, um, New Zealand Bill of Rights Acts has very important principles. Yes. But there's also, I guess some of the concerns have been raised about this bill currently we're considering. Yeah. And as a section with our committee is that there are a broader range of public policy issues that this may constrain, but that isn't that the same issue with the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act, that there's a broader range of public policy issues that may be difficult to reconcile,

that therefore Parliament does have the opportunity to, and does ignore reports of inconsistency from the Attorney General? Um, well, yes, but the courts don't. Uh, and that's the difference. The trouble with the regulatory responsibility bill is that the courts are written out of it all together except where the warrants are to be ordered to get material for this strange committee that is being set up.

Uh, and, and the, the, I don't see that as the same thing. You to have a statute passed that is law for some purposes and not per us, is a very novel provision. And it's received very little attention by the people who examined this bill because the economists don't understand law.

That's a defining aspect of being an economist. So I just think that you really do need to look at what the Law Society said about the bill. The Law Society gave it a real, and it really deserves it. And, and to, to certain ignore that is, is really worrying.

I'll come to Dr. Nan. Thank you so much, chair, and thank you so much, uh, CVI Palmer for being with us today. Um, one of the things that this committee is really grappling with, and I think I've said that and, and you know, previously we've heard as well, is where do we fit within this new structure? Uh, and I think there are two aspects for that.

Number one, like, as you pointed out, and maybe the simple solution is in RSB, secondary legislation should just not be included. That's right. And that make it really clean. Yeah. But if it were to retain the way it is right now for both primary and secondary, from our perspective, what we haven't been able to get clarity on is, number one, the overlap between the roles.

Who has the final say? Is it RRC or is it the regulatory standards board being one part of my question. And the second part of my question is, hold on. Can we start With that? Can we do it one at a time? Yep. Uh, the regulatory standards board is a creature of the minister, and it is not a creature of parliament.

This committee is a creature of parliament and members of this committee have the right to move regulations to be dis. Mm-hmm. And they can be, and that seems to me to be the answer to most of the difficulties that arise with secondary legislation.

And, and, and that, that is a very important thing for the committee to grasp. Because if you don't have a purpose, you will cease to exist. The committee was much bigger. I seem to recall when we started, it's, it's quite small now, and I doubt whether the members want to serve on it much. 'cause it probably doesn't have glamor, uh, even though it's important.

Uh, and, and, and the difficulty with that is that you put there to do a job to protect the public. And, and, and the executive is not the final arbital the house is, but you have power in that to move motions. Yeah, that's right. And, and that's not something that the, that the strange body that they're gonna set up, they don't, can't do that.

But what they can do is to like leaves and autumn put them on ministers and make the ministers very nervous. Dr. Nan, do you have a second? Yeah. So, um, thank you so much. And I think it is true, I might, I'm not an official member of this committee. I'm here purely for the enjoyment of it. Well, you ought to get on it. Oh, I love it. Um, Dr. Sorry, the second question is, uh, around the fact that this, regardless of what it's, this committee looks to build consensus in most.

Yes. Yes. Always has done And always has done. And he looks and it's cross party, you know? Yes. 'cause like you said, it's Committee of Parliament, but what we're seeing is the lack of independence and potentially transparency with what is considered a comparable board as well. Do you see that there is a possibility where that, how this committee does things is able to be translated into what the regulatory standard board could possibly be as something that is independently appointed as opposed to No being part of the minister.

It's either a parliamentary body or it isn't. Okay. Uh, and, and it has no legitimacy, but isn't, yeah. Uh, and, and the legitimacy, uh, comes from being in the supreme rule making body, and you are the guardian of ensuring that the law, making power is not abused by the executive, and the executive can abuse it.

Uh, they used to abuse it a great deal by trying to ensure that deemed regulations, I don't know, you know, anything about the history of deemed regulations, but that was a terrific issue for many, many years. I think it's gone away more or less now. But you see, it's, it's the failure to, to do things properly that this committee is, exists to, to prevent and stop.

And there hasn't been enough stopping. Mm. And, and, and, and that is because ministers who design regulations don't want them to allow, and so they won't vote for that. Yeah. And often the, the ministers have the power in the house. That's the problem. Mm.

And, and so I think that the, the bipartisan thing that is necessary to make the committee work properly needs to extend the back benches from the governing party, feeling that they can exercise their judgment without fear or favor. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Because that is the way you see, coming back to the regulatory standards, bill, I heard the minister on the, uh, on a podcast once say that they would all be the, the members of this, uh, council, whatever it's called, uh, would all be economists.

Well, that would not be appropriate, because if you don't believe in regulation, and a lot of economists just want unright of capitalism, but regulation is designed to have some checks and balances against unbridled capitalism. That is its purpose. We have to have capitalism.

Of course not, some people don't think that, but it's the only way to generate wealth in any effective way. But you can't have it without controls on it. And and that's really at the essence of this whole debate. And, and it's, it's, it's a debate that's got off on the wrong tack.

And I thought that's why the officials didn't agree with their own minister, even though their ministry had only been recently set up. They didn't agree with him. And he's got another line of it. And, and I, I don't, can't see that the parliament should count.

They, you need to have harmony with both these things. The point should have been made since I've been here by you are quite right. You have a responsibility. It's an important responsibility and you ought to exercise it, but it needs to be gelled into the system because you, it's not sitting out there in left field somewhere.

And, and I just, I just cannot understand how the parliament ever got itself into this position. Mm-hmm. I'll come, um, a quick question from Miles Anderson and then whenever she Walters a quick question from me and then we'll be at time. So Jeffrey, Be pardon? We, We've only got time for three more questions. That's all right. So, um, I just want you to walk us back to the time this committee was formed.

Yes. Mm-hmm. Um, and that would be, um, in the 84 85 8. Yes. That, that period. Yes. And through that period, were there any disallowance then? Uh, as far as I can remember, there's only been three or four ever. So, but, but, but I think that could have been, Because what we do do, which in defense of this committee Yeah.

Quite often we identify poorly written, um, secondary legislation and then that's then ti it up and referred back to us. Yes. That that's quite right. And you've done a lot of that. And the thing is, you don't have to disallow it if you've corrected it, do you? Correct. Yes. And, and, and that's gone on.

And that was the purpose. But, but you see the, the, the, the purpose of the authors of the regulator responsibility bill don't seem to think that that does any good. It does a lot of good. And that's the, the jurisprudence of this committee is long, it's 20 or 30 years long. And, and, and it's used and it's especially used by government departments.

'cause they are the ones who cop the criticism from the committee. Mm. And, and, and so they should and, and, and so I think that's great work. But that seems to me to be the main explanation for why Moore haven't been disallowed. That's right. I think, um, by identifying for secondary Yeah.

Legislation and it's generally amended to fix that Yeah. Um, issue, which then leads the question why you think we should be disa allowing more? Well, well, well, if the case is that we're overregulated, is it because of the regulation of the secondary legislation or the primary legislation then and from the take, take the economic stabilization act as a classic example, I, that was certainly excessive and should have been, should have.

Not in a free society, you can't have an act that, uh, you just can't. And, and, and, um, the way we haven't got many acts like that now, but, but the, the enthusiasm for terrific deregulation makes me nervous. I don't quite know where that desire comes from because it, the evidence has not been put in front of this parliament about why it, how it is.

It's asserted, but it's not generated as evidence anywhere that I have seen. Mm-hmm. P Walters, thank you and thank you committee for allowing me to ask a question. I'm also not a regular member. I'm just here to having a chat with you, Jeffrey. Oh, okay. Um, I can feel your exasperation. Yeah.

Um, and I think firstly a comment, one thing that there isn't a lot of commentary on is the deeply constitutional thing that the bill does, which is insert the manner and form provision that requires the statement of consistency Yes. To be, um, to be filed along with the introduction of the bill.

Yes. Um, which I think the clerk spoke to, but yes, my other concern, The Clarks uh, Clarks submission submission I read makes a very good point. And, and I, and I guess my question for you is I think there's another interesting constitutional issue that sits between the Bill of Rights Act and this bill. Um, so if things remain as they are, there was an argument at select committee that the bill itself is discriminatory or indirectly discriminatory.

No, I agree with That. Um, and so if that's the case and, uh, the ability of secondary ledge remains, um, open to being interpreted in accordance with the principles. Yep. Could a declaration of inconsistency case be brought against the bill as it passes into legislation? Because both, both, both point to each other in many ways.

Um, and my sense is that There's a bit of a constitutional black hole almost sense point. Well, the, the parliament, the, the courts are not going to, uh, interfere with legislation. I was going through parliament that they will not do no, uh, and, and cannot do. Mm-hmm. Uh, and there's plenty of recent authority of that cook said it 10 or 15 years ago, but should it pass into law?

Well, if it passes into law, that's different. And when it's passed into law, I don't think there's any chance of avoiding legal cases then despite what the bill wishes to do, it isn't going to work. It's a piece of, um, it's a piece of we don't like the courts because we think the courts might not do what we want, so we won't allow them into this.

And I find that deeply unconstitutional because we have three branches of government and the great protection for the ordinary people. And that is that you have the executive, you have the legislature, and you have the judiciary, and they all have a role to play in these things. And you cannot write the judiciary out of it and call yourself a democratic society.

Mm-hmm. Um, so Jeffrey, we are at time, let me ask you a quick question. We find ourselves in the position where we are one of the only two cross partisan, genuinely cross partisan, uh, committees in the Parliament. And, uh, both of those committees are called to determine what will be in the standing orders at the end of this parliament.

Yeah. About the role of Regulations review committee. The other is the Standing Orders Committee. Um, as the chair, I would like to progress usefully for the standing Orders committee, what the role of the Regulations review Committee Yes. Is now, um, we are, we are struggling to find consensus. Give us some advice about how we should embrace that initial sense of bipartisanship with which you established this committee.

Well, I think if you took the l d's first point about keeping the statutes separate from the, from the, uh, secondary legislation, that's the beginning of wisdom on the point, because the statutes are the ones that cause the problem really.

Uh, and, and when it gets down to secondary legislation, it's, it's not, it's not as bad. Even though there's much more of it, it's not as deeply offensive as such provisions sometimes. I mean, it used to be put in statutes that the minister's decision will be fun and you know, that the minister can decide.

And he likes all that sort of, you can't do that anymore. But they, I've seen it done and, and it hasn't been done for a while, but, but I, we have tolerated strong, powerful, unified central government in this country for a long time. The Australians don't have anything like that. Mm.
Okay. Thank you. Um, thank you so much for joining us. Thank you for being so generous. Well, I Hope I wasn't a trouble.
